Research Explainer  ·  Reinforcement Learning

Teaching a Computer to be a Smart Bouncer for your APIs

A plain-English walkthrough of a research project that uses AI to manage internet traffic — deciding which requests get served, which wait, and which get turned away — all while staying within a limited computing budget.

No prior ML knowledge needed
Real experimental charts included
Limitations discussed
The Basics The AI Brain Cost Awareness Reward Design Results Real World Limitations
Section 01

What Problem Are We Solving?

Imagine you run a popular restaurant. On a normal Tuesday, 30 customers walk in and you comfortably seat them all. But on New Year's Eve, 300 people show up at once. You can't serve everyone — so you need a system: a bouncer at the door who decides who gets in, how fast, and in what order.

In the world of software, APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are the doors to online services. When you open an app, it sends dozens of tiny requests — "fetch my messages," "check my balance," "load this image" — to remote servers. Those servers can only handle so many requests at once. Without control, they get overwhelmed and crash.

Key Term — API Gateway

An API Gateway is the digital bouncer. It sits in front of a server and controls which incoming requests are allowed through, how many per second, and in what order. This project is about making that bouncer smarter using AI.

The Problem with Old-School Bouncers

Traditional rate limiters use fixed rules — for example: "allow a maximum of 12 requests per second, full stop." These rules work fine on a calm Tuesday, but they struggle when:

Example — Fixed Rules Failing

A shopping website normally gets 100 requests/second. It sets a hard limit of 150. On Black Friday, 5,000 requests arrive per second. The fixed limiter blocks most of them — including urgent payment confirmations — treating them the same as low-priority image loads.


Result: customers see payment failures even though the server could have prioritized those requests and processed them fine.

Old Approach
The Rigid Bouncer

"The list says max 12 people. I don't care if one of them is the CEO having a cardiac event — rules are rules."

This Project's Approach
The Intelligent Bouncer

"12 people is the guideline, but I'll adapt. I see payment requests piling up — I'll let those through first and hold the low-priority ones."

This research project builds that "intelligent bouncer" using a technique from AI called Reinforcement Learning.

Section 02

The AI Brain — Reinforcement Learning & PPO

So how do you teach a computer to make smart admission decisions? You could write thousands of rules by hand. Or you could let the AI learn by doing — which is exactly what Reinforcement Learning (RL) does.

What is Reinforcement Learning?

Think of training a dog. You don't explain the rules in English — you give treats when it does something right and say "no" when it doesn't. Over thousands of repetitions, it figures out what behavior earns treats. Reinforcement Learning is the same idea, but for software. The AI tries actions, gets "rewards" or "penalties," and slowly learns what to do.

The Three Core Concepts

01
State — What does the agent observe?

At every moment, the AI looks at the current situation: how many requests are waiting, how long they've been waiting, how many were recently dropped, what the current traffic level is, and the priority mix of waiting requests.

02
Action — What can the agent do?

The AI has exactly three choices: decrease the rate limit, hold it steady, or increase it. These change the limit in steps of 2, within a range of 2 to 18 requests per step.

03
Reward — How does it know if it did well?

After each action, the environment scores the outcome. High throughput earns positive points. High latency, dropped requests, and especially dropped high-priority requests earn penalties. The AI learns to maximize its total score over time.

Why PPO Specifically?

PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) is a popular, stable RL algorithm. Think of it like a student who learns cautiously — it makes small, measured improvements each time rather than wild swings. This prevents the AI from "forgetting" good strategies while trying new ones.

Watching the AI Learn — Count-Based Training Curves

The chart below shows all four metrics being tracked across 260 training episodes for the count-based (original) PPO. Think of each episode as one complete "game" the AI plays against simulated traffic.

Figure — Count-Based PPO Training Progress (260 Episodes)
How to read this: Each sub-plot tracks one metric over 260 training episodes (x-axis).

Episode Reward (top-left, green): The total score the AI earned in each episode. Notice it starts negative and noisy, but gradually trends upward toward 0.5–0.8 as the AI learns. The continued volatility is normal — traffic scenarios vary randomly each episode.

Average Latency (top-right, red): How long requests wait before being served. Early in training, latency spikes wildly (the AI is experimenting recklessly). By episode ~50 it stabilises, mostly staying below 2–3 units.

Throughput (bottom-left, blue): Requests served per step. The AI quickly learns to keep this near the server capacity of 12. The occasional dips to 4–5 correspond to high-load episodes where the AI is still learning to manage overload.

Drop Rate (bottom-right, brown): Fraction of requests rejected. Drops sharply from 0.4 in early episodes to near 0 as the AI learns not to admit more than it can serve.

The Training Scenarios

🌿 Low Load

Traffic below capacity. Easy to manage, but the AI must not over-admit and waste resources.

🔥 High Load

Sustained overload. Requests pour in faster than the server can handle. The AI must triage aggressively.

⚡ Bursty

Normal traffic with sudden spikes. The AI must react quickly to brief overloads.

〰️ Oscillating

Traffic rises and falls in a wave pattern. The AI must anticipate cycles rather than just react.

How the Count-Based AI Performed in High Load

After training, the AI was evaluated against traditional fixed-rule approaches. Here are the results from the most challenging scenario — high load — where requests arrive faster than the server can handle.

Figure — High Load Throughput (Count-Based)
The PPO AI (teal bar, leftmost) achieves 12.00 requests/step — matching the server's maximum capacity, same as the best fixed rules. The critical outlier is adaptive at just 2.11 — it became so paranoid about overload that it barely admits any traffic at all, demonstrating how easy it is to tune a controller too conservatively.
Figure — High Priority Success Rate (Count-Based)
This shows what fraction of high-priority requests (e.g., payments) successfully got served. PPO achieves 89% — matching fixed and priority heuristic, and beating the token bucket's 90% by a hair. The adaptive controller again collapses at 69%, confirming it sacrifices useful work to avoid overload. This chart is why the paper reduced the high-priority penalty weight — the AI needed to stay at ~89%, not obsess over it.
Section 03

The New Idea — Cost-Aware Admission

The original rate limiter treated all requests as equal units. But in reality, a simple lookup query takes milliseconds while a large AI inference request takes much more compute. This project's key innovation: replace the simple "12 requests per step" limit with a processing budget of 24 cost units per step. Each request carries a cost between 1 and 5.

The Insight

The server can process as many requests as fit within that budget — meaning two cheap requests (cost 1 each = 2 units) are treated differently from one expensive request (cost 5 units), even though both involve one admission decision.

Example — Cost Budget vs. Fixed Count

Old system (fixed count = 12/step): 12 image-generation requests arrive, each costing 5 units. Serves all 12 — total cost 60 units, way beyond real capacity. Server crashes.


New system (budget = 24 cost units): Same 12 arrive. Can only serve 4 (4×5=20 ≤ 24). Also fits one cheap request (cost 3, total=23). Five requests served total — server stays healthy.


Or conversely: 24 cheap requests arrive (cost 1 each). All 24 fit within budget — much higher throughput than before!

Section 04

The Hardest Part — Getting the Reward Right

This section is the heart of the research. Defining what "good" means for the AI turned out to be the most critical challenge — and getting it wrong caused a spectacular failure.

Attempt 1: The Over-Conservative Count-Based Reward

The first reward formula heavily penalised dropped high-priority requests (weight 3.1×). The AI became so paranoid about ever missing a high-priority request that it throttled everything down. After fixing that weight to 1.0×, performance recovered dramatically.

reward = + throughput_score
- latency_penalty
- drop_rate_penalty
- high_priority_drop_penalty ← was 3.1× (way too high)
- overload_indicator

Attempt 2: The Naive Cost-Aware Reward — and Its Collapse

When the project added cost awareness, the team kept the old request-count objective and just added a small bonus for serving expensive requests. This caused a catastrophic failure. The training curves below show exactly what happened:

Figure — Naive Cost-Aware PPO: Training Collapse
This is the "crime scene" of the project — a textbook example of reward misalignment. Six metrics are tracked across 260 episodes.

Episode Reward (top-left): Rises healthily until around episode 130, then crashes hard to −1.5 and never recovers. The dashed line shows the exact moment of collapse.

Throughput (middle-left, blue): Was averaging 7–8 requests/step. After episode 130, it collapses to just 2 requests/step — the AI learned to barely admit anything.

Drop Rate (middle-right, brown): Explodes from ~30% to 80–85% after collapse — ironically, the AI trying to avoid penalties creates the worst drop rate possible.

Processed Cost (bottom-left, purple): Crashes from ~23 cost units/step to only 6 — leaving 75% of the available budget unused.

Budget Utilization (bottom-right, orange): Plummets from near 100% to just 27% — a 73-percentage-point waste of capacity.

Why? The environment judged performance by cost units processed, but the AI was rewarded mainly for request count. These two objectives pulled in opposite directions, and the AI found a "safe" local minimum: admit almost nothing, avoid all penalties, but be completely useless.
⚠️ The Core Lesson

Merely adding a cost bonus to an existing count-based reward is not enough. If the dominant objective still measures the wrong thing, the AI exploits that misalignment. You must replace the wrong objective, not just augment it.

Attempt 3: Cost-Only Reward — The Fix

The solution was to remove the request-count term entirely and make served cost the primary positive signal. Now the AI's optimization target perfectly matched the environment's constraint.

reward = + served_cost_score ← replaces request count entirely
- latency_penalty
- drop_rate_penalty
- high_priority_drop_penalty
- overload_indicator
Section 05

How Well Did It Work? — The Experimental Results

After the reward fix, the researchers compared their cost-aware PPO against every baseline across multiple traffic scenarios. Let's walk through the actual charts from the paper.

High Load — The Critical Stress Test

This is the hardest standard scenario: requests arrive faster than the server can handle, sustained. All four charts below compare PPO against all baselines during high load.

Figure — High Load: Throughput
PPO serves 7.80 requests/step — comparable to most heuristics (7.69–7.89 range). The adaptive baseline collapses again at 2.11, confirming it's systematically over-cautious. The key point: PPO reaches this throughput without any hand-coded rules — it learned it entirely from experience.
Figure — High Load: Budget Utilization
PPO uses 97% of the 24-unit budget — nearly identical to the best heuristics (96–98%). Only adaptive fails again at 26%. This chart is the proof that the cost-only reward worked: the AI learned to pack the budget aggressively, not just count requests.
Figure — High Load: Latency
PPO's latency is 3.23 units — higher than token bucket (1.62) and the priority heuristics (~2.07–2.12), but far lower than always_max (8.38) and cost_efficiency (8.34). The heuristics win here by being more conservative about admissions. PPO's slightly higher latency is a direct trade-off: it admits more aggressively to fill the budget, which causes some queue build-up.
Figure — High Load: Served Cost
This is the metric PPO was directly optimizing for. It achieves 23.40 cost units/step — nearly matching the theoretical budget of 24. All good baselines cluster at 23.1–23.6, showing they also fill the budget well. The stark outlier is adaptive at 6.35 — it's leaving 73% of processing capacity completely idle. PPO delivers near-maximum useful computational work.

Out-of-Distribution Tests — Cheap Flood

These tests use traffic the AI never saw during training — the real measure of generalization. The "cheap flood" scenario floods the gateway with many tiny, low-cost requests (cost ~1 each). Since these pack efficiently into the 24-unit budget, many can be served simultaneously.

Figure — Cheap Flood: Throughput
PPO achieves 15.66 requests/step — close to the best performing baselines (always_max: 16.06, cost_efficiency: 16.04). This is much higher than normal high-load throughput (7.8) because cheap requests pack tightly into the budget. PPO generalised well to this unseen scenario — it understood the underlying principle of "fill the budget" even with an unfamiliar cost distribution.
Figure — Cheap Flood: Budget Utilization
PPO uses 77% of the budget — slightly below most baselines at 78%. The striking outlier is adaptive at just 25%, which completely fails this scenario. The fact that PPO matches 6 out of 7 competitors demonstrates robust generalization to the cheap-flood distribution shift, even without ever training on it.

Out-of-Distribution Tests — Expensive Attack

The "expensive attack" scenario sends a few extremely costly, high-priority requests that each consume a large chunk of the 24-unit budget on their own. This is the scenario where everything fails.

Figure — Expensive Attack: Latency (All Systems Struggle)
Every single system fails on this scenario — including the heuristics. Latencies across the board range from 39 to 43 units (compared to 1–3 under normal conditions). This is 10–15× worse than normal operation.

PPO's latency (39.38) is actually among the lowest here — slightly better than token_bucket (40.89), priority_heuristic (40.89), cost_aware_priority (41.14), and cost_efficiency (43.11). The real damage to PPO was its extremely low throughput (1.7 requests/step) and near-zero high-priority success rate (12.5%) in this scenario, visible in the paper's tables.

Why does everything fail? When requests cost 5+ units each and only 4–5 fit in the 24-unit budget per step, congestion builds faster than any rule or learned policy can drain it. No system trained or designed for costs of 1–5 can gracefully handle the unexpected arrival of many maximum-cost requests simultaneously.
Key Takeaway from Expensive Attack

The expensive attack scenario is not a failure of PPO specifically — it's a fundamental limitation of training on one cost distribution and deploying into another. It reveals that the AI (and the heuristics!) learned the scale of costs seen during training, not an abstract, cost-distribution-agnostic strategy for budget packing.

Summary: Where PPO Stands Relative to Heuristics

ScenarioBest Heuristic RewardPPO RewardVerdict
High Load120.2 (CostAwarePriority)102.0Heuristic wins
Bursty167.1 (PriorityHeuristic)164.4Very close — AI competitive
Oscillating167.3 (PriorityHeuristic)157.8Heuristic wins
Cheap Flood171.3 (PPO itself is near best)171.3PPO matches best
Expensive Attack−1199 (all fail)−1203All systems fail equally
Section 06

A Real-World Scenario Where This Matters

🏦 Case Study — Digital Banking Platform

A Bank's API Gateway During Peak Trading Hours

Imagine a major bank with a mobile app used by 5 million customers. Their backend API serves three very different types of requests — each with radically different costs and priorities.

🔴 High Priority · High Cost
Payment transactions, loan approvals, fraud checks. Cost: 4–5 units. Cannot drop.
🟡 Medium Priority · Medium Cost
Account balance checks, transaction history. Cost: 2–3 units. Delay acceptable.
🟢 Low Priority · Low Cost
Push notifications, analytics pings, background sync. Cost: 1 unit. Droppable.

The Crisis: Month-End Processing Day

On the last day of the month, salaries are credited simultaneously. 2 million customers open the app at the same time — 10× normal load. Without smart rate limiting:

Without Smart Rate Limiting — The Disaster

• A fixed limiter lets in requests up to a count of 12,000/sec regardless of cost
• Background analytics floods the queue first (thousands of cheap pings)
• Budget exhausted by low-value work
• Real payment confirmations queue for 40+ seconds
• Customers think transactions failed and retry — making things worse
• Server crashes under the retry storm — the bank's busiest day becomes its worst outage

With This Project's Cost-Aware PPO System

01
AI observes the state

Sees: queue growing fast, mean queued cost rising (expensive payment requests piling up), high-priority drop rate spiking. Budget utilization near 100%.

02
AI decreases the rate limit

Reduces overall admissions to protect budget. Low-priority analytics pings get rejected first (designed to be retried later anyway).

03
Budget now serves valuable work

The 24-unit budget goes toward payment confirmations (cost 5 each → 4–5 per step). Payments process within 2 seconds. Customers are happy.

04
Load normalizes, AI adapts

As the spike subsides, the AI gradually increases the rate limit again, allowing analytics and notifications to resume. No crash, no customer complaints.

The Bottom Line

In a production banking system, this approach could mean the difference between a seamless payday experience and a widely-reported outage. The AI optimizes for computational value delivered — which maps directly to business outcomes.

Section 07

Where This Work Falls Short

Any honest research must acknowledge what it doesn't solve. Here are the key limitations — some discussed in the paper, some additional ones that apply broadly:

Limitation 01
Poor Generalization to Unseen Cost Distributions

The AI was trained on request costs of 1–5. The expensive attack scenario exposed that it learned the training scale, not an abstract cost-packing concept. As shown in the latency chart, every system fails equally badly under this shift.

Limitation 02
Heuristics Remain Difficult to Beat

Hand-crafted rules outperformed PPO in high-load and oscillating scenarios. The AI paid a sample-efficiency tax for discovering what an engineer could encode in one rule.

Limitation 03
Discrete, Limited Action Space

Only three actions — decrease, hold, increase — in fixed steps of 2. A real system might benefit from continuous control or per-class admission policies.

Limitation 04
No Online Adaptation

Once trained, the AI's behavior is frozen. If traffic patterns shift permanently, the system can't adapt without full retraining from scratch.

Limitation 05
Simulation vs. Reality Gap

The system is trained in a simplified simulator. Real networks have packet jitter, TCP retransmissions, cascading failures, and hardware variability the simulator ignores.

Limitation 06
Single Gateway — No Coordination

The research models one isolated gateway. Large systems have hundreds of gateways. A policy that works for one node may create bottlenecks elsewhere without coordination.

Limitation 07
No Hard Safety Guarantees

The AI optimizes a reward signal, but gives no formal guarantees. Production systems must meet SLOs — e.g., "99.9% of payments complete in <3 seconds" — which the current approach cannot enforce.

Limitation 08
Fine-Tuning Hurts Robustness

Specializing the model on high-load scenarios improved in-distribution performance slightly, but degraded the expensive-attack scenario further — a classic case of catastrophic forgetting.

Suggested Future Directions (from the paper)

Online adaptation to new cost regimes · Multi-gateway coordination · Explicit safety constraint layers · Richer feature engineering · Combining learned policies with structural priors about cost packing rather than pure model-free RL.

Summary

Putting It All Together

This project set out to answer: can a learning AI manage API traffic better than static rules, and can it reason about the computational cost of each request?

The answer is a qualified yes. The AI successfully learned to:

✓
Use the budget efficiently

Reaching ~97% budget utilization in high-load scenarios — after the naive reward collapsed to just 26%.

✓
Prioritize valuable work

Maintaining ~88% high-priority success rates under heavy load while serving near-maximum computational cost units.

✓
Generalize to some new situations

Handling the cheap-flood OOD scenario gracefully — matching heuristics it was never designed to compete with.

!
But with real limits

Failing on the expensive-attack distribution shift (alongside all baselines), unable to consistently beat the best hand-crafted heuristics, and with no formal safety guarantees.

The deepest lesson isn't about rate limiting — it's about AI design. What you reward the AI for matters more than which algorithm you use. A misaligned reward silently guides the AI toward the wrong solution, and the failure can look deceptively sensible — until you examine the training curves and see the collapse.

Explainer based on: "Cost-Aware Adaptive Rate Limiting with PPO" · Harshal Rudra · May 2026
Written for a general audience. All charts are actual experimental results from the paper.
